methodology: For each critical finding, attempt to disprove it. Check if upstream guards prevent the condition. Confirm or downgrade.

critical_findings_verified.adversarial_challenge: Does middleware protect /api/kpi? Middleware matcher includes '/api/kpi/:path*' (middleware.ts:79). This matches /api/kpi routes. Middleware checks session via supabase.auth.getSession(). If no session, redirects to /auth.
critical_findings_verified.counterargument: Middleware provides session authentication. Unauthenticated users are redirected. The 'zero authentication' claim is FALSE for browser-based attacks.
critical_findings_verified.remaining_risk: BUT: (1) Middleware only checks session existence, not admin role. Any authenticated user (creator, intern, viewer) can call PUT /api/kpi and see ALL aggregated business metrics. (2) Middleware returns a redirect (307), not a 401 — API clients may not follow redirects. (3) The endpoint aggregates all creators into memory (PERF-001) and returns sensitive breakdowns.
critical_findings_verified.reason: Authentication exists via middleware, but authorization (admin role check) is missing. Any authenticated user sees all data.
critical_findings_verified.recommended_fix: Add admin role check at top of PUT handler: check session.user.user_metadata?.role === 'admin'. Return 403 if not admin.

critical_findings_verified.adversarial_challenge: Is service_role key usage actually dangerous if middleware enforces session checks? Could RLS be irrelevant if app-level auth is solid?
critical_findings_verified.counterargument: Middleware DOES check sessions for most routes. Routes like /api/kpi, /api/creators, /api/samples all go through middleware. So the 'any API route compromise = full DB exposure' scenario requires bypassing middleware first.
critical_findings_verified.remaining_risk: (1) Middleware is NOT a security boundary for API routes — it returns redirects, not 401s. Programmatic API clients ignore redirects. (2) /api/sync skips middleware entirely (middleware.ts:28). (3) The service_role key is a single point of failure — if leaked, ALL data is exposed regardless of RLS. (4) RLS policies with USING(true) are still meaningless for client-side Supabase usage. (5) Any new API route added without middleware protection inherits full DB access.
critical_findings_verified.reason: Service_role bypass + USING(true) RLS = no defense-in-depth. Middleware helps but doesn't fix the fundamental issue. New routes inherit full access by default.
critical_findings_verified.recommended_fix: Architectural: Create two Supabase clients (anon + service_role). Use service_role ONLY for cron/admin operations after explicit role verification. Implement proper RLS with auth.uid() checks. This is a multi-sprint effort.

critical_findings_verified.adversarial_challenge: Does the singleton actually cause cross-request data leakage? createServerClient() with service_role key has { auth: { persistSession: false } }. Does Supabase client cache user context?
critical_findings_verified.counterargument: With persistSession: false, the Supabase client does NOT cache auth sessions. Service_role key bypasses RLS entirely, so there's no per-user filtering. The client is stateless — it doesn't remember which user made the previous request.
critical_findings_verified.remaining_risk: The singleton IS an anti-pattern in serverless, but the actual data leakage risk is LOW because: (1) service_role bypasses RLS, so all queries return ALL data regardless of user. (2) The API routes already determine which creator's data to return based on request params, not client state. (3) persistSession: false prevents auth state caching.
critical_findings_verified.reason: The singleton is architecturally wrong but doesn't cause cross-request data leakage because the Supabase client is stateless with service_role + persistSession: false. The real risk is if the codebase ever switches to user-scoped RLS — the singleton would then be dangerous.
critical_findings_verified.recommended_fix: Still worth fixing (factory per request), but not urgent. Priority below idempotency and transaction fixes.

critical_findings_verified.adversarial_challenge: Can the partial failure scenario actually happen? If INSERT referral succeeds but RPC fails, how bad is the inconsistency?
critical_findings_verified.counterargument: The RPC failure at line 148 only logs an error (console.error) and does NOT throw. The function continues successfully. So the referral IS created, but uses_count is NOT incremented. This inconsistency is silent.
critical_findings_verified.remaining_risk: (1) referral_codes.uses_count becomes inaccurate. (2) If uses_count is used for rate limiting (e.g., max uses per code), the limit is never enforced. (3) STRUCT-002 makes this WORSE: the RPC writes updated_at to referral_codes, but that column doesn't exist in schema-v3. So the RPC ALWAYS fails in production. Every single referral signup silently fails to increment uses_count.
critical_findings_verified.reason: The RPC fails silently every time (schema column mismatch). Combined with STRUCT-002, every referral signup creates inconsistent state. Transaction fix alone won't help — must also fix schema.
critical_findings_verified.recommended_fix: FIX STRUCT-002 FIRST (add updated_at column), then wrap in transaction RPC. Fix order: STRUCT-002 → ARCH-003.

critical_findings_verified.adversarial_challenge: Do the cron schedules actually overlap at 3AM? Analyze vercel.json + GET handler logic.
critical_findings_verified.counterargument_for_overlap: vercel.json: '0 3 * * *' (3AM daily) and '0 */6 * * *' (0,6,12,18 UTC). At 3AM: ONLY the first cron fires. The second cron fires at 0,6,12,18 — NOT at 3. GET handler: if (hour===3) → full sync, else if (hour%6===0) → shop orders. At 3AM, only full sync runs. NO overlap at 3AM between the two crons.
critical_findings_verified.remaining_risk_idempotency: ARCH-007 (cron overlap at 3AM) is PARTIALLY FALSE — code logic prevents double execution. BUT: (1) If Vercel retries the 3AM cron due to timeout/error, runFullSync calls syncShopOrders AGAIN with the same 7-day window. (2) Manual POST /api/sync {type:'full'} also reprocesses everything. (3) syncShopOrders has NO order_id deduplication — same orders processed again → increment_creator_gmv called twice → GMV doubled.
critical_findings_verified.verdict: CONFIRMED critical for ARCH-004 (no idempotency) and ARCH-008 (no order dedup). DOWNGRADE ARCH-007 to medium (theoretical overlap prevented by code logic, but no retry protection).
critical_findings_verified.reason: While cron schedules don't actually overlap at 3AM, retries and manual triggers WILL double-process orders without idempotency keys or order deduplication.
critical_findings_verified.recommended_fix: Create order_tracking table with UNIQUE(shop_order_id). Check before processing. Add cron_sync_state for run deduplication.

severity_changes.reason: Middleware provides session auth, but lacks admin role check
Singleton is anti-pattern but doesn't cause data leakage due to stateless service_role client
Code logic prevents actual cron overlap at 3AM; risk is only on retries (covered by ARCH-004)

confirmed_criticals: UNI-001: Service role key bypasses ALL RLS (architectural, multi-sprint fix)
ARCH-003: No transaction boundaries + STRUCT-002 schema mismatch = every referral signup silently corrupts data
ARCH-004: No idempotency + no order dedup = GMV doubling on retry/manual trigger
fix_dependency_ordering: 1. STRUCT-001 + STRUCT-002 — Add missing updated_at columns (prerequisite for ARCH-003 and sync-orchestrator)
2. ARCH-004 + ARCH-008 — Order dedup + idempotency (prevents ongoing GMV corruption)
3. SEC-1 (now high) — Add admin role check to /api/kpi PUT
6. ARCH-003 — Transaction boundaries (after STRUCT-002 fixes schema)
7. UNI-001 — RLS architecture overhaul (multi-sprint, not auto-fixable)
auto_fixable_in_phase3: STRUCT-001: Add updated_at to content_tracking schema
STRUCT-002: Add updated_at to referral_codes + referrals schema
SEC-1: Add admin role check to /api/kpi PUT + GET + POST
BUG-1: Add field validation + status enum check to /api/shipping POST
BUG-2: Add proper null/error handling to /api/kpi PUT
BUG-4: Guard division by zero in weeks_to_30k
CFG-001: Fix cron timezone comment (documentation fix)
deferred_to_manual_review: UNI-001: RLS architecture overhaul — requires design decisions on role model
ARCH-002: Singleton removal — safe refactor but touches many files
ARCH-003: Transaction boundaries — requires new RPC functions
ARCH-004: Idempotency system — requires new table + code changes
SEC-2: Rate limiting — requires Upstash or similar external dependency
